input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10th‑scale autonomous vehicle that serves as the physical twin for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) study. The vehicle is a teleoperated platform retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, comprising a lower and upper chassis layer and a set of autonomy elements. Its hardware constraints include resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECU) that lack sufficient compute, making it an ideal candidate for off‑loading experiments. The system operates in a real‑world driving domain, capturing vehicle state data for ACC evaluation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system delivers three primary services: 1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that displays real‑time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows operators to view, save, and generate reports on ACC‑relevant data; 2) Computation Offloading – the entire ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation, and downstream actuation command generation) is executed on cloud compute resources and the resulting desired speed is transmitted back to the vehicle; 3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable or disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands via low end‑to‑end latency communication, while developers can troubleshoot using the DT’s data collection and monitoring capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built upon cloud‑based compute resources for ACC off‑loading, a low‑latency communication link (ensuring safety‑critical performance), and a middleware stack that exchanges data between the vehicle’s ECU and the DT. The system includes a Digital Twin Entity, the physical twin components, and a UI frontend that aggregates data collection, reporting, and remote command functionalities, following best practices for a Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human‑in‑the‑Loop.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital twin’s temporal coordination is driven by a 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy requirement. All computing nodes and physical devices must share a common notion of time so that data and control signals are exchanged within a 100 ms envelope. The system does not specify higher‑frequency sync loops, but it acknowledges that stricter (<100 ms) synchronization is mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards for which the platform would need to support if required. Latency constraints for individual messaging are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The description does not explicitly detail the number of digital twin instances or a centralized versus decentralized architecture beyond noting that the Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User entities are deployed on distinct hardware components. No mention of multiple twin instances or formal coordination mechanisms between separate twin deployments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping each software feature entity (FE) to a ROS 2 package that contains one or more ROS 2 nodes. The system is organized into three hierarchical entities: User Entity (contains a User Interface FE split into a 3D Visualization and Control Panel), Digital Twin Entity (hosts data management and control mechanisms), and Physical Twin Entity (hosts device communication, data pre‑processing, actuation, and general automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering & tracking). Each entity is mapped to a distinct physical node, providing decentralized deployment while maintaining a coherent whole through ROS 2 message passing. Resource management is achieved by assigning specific FEs to dedicated hardware platforms; for example, the Physical Twin Entity sits on the vehicle’s compute unit, whereas the User Entity runs on a separate workstation. System‑wide coordination is performed via ROS 2 topics, services, and actions that implement the data flow between sensors, actuators, and digital‑twin logic. The architecture departs from the ISO 23247 standard in that the Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources (compute, sensors, actuators) rather than being an abstract entity; this enables hosting of automotive‑specific functionality.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensors on the vehicle (LiDAR, on‑board cameras, vehicle state buses) generate raw point cloud data (XYZ coordinates with timestamps, reflectance), vehicle kinematic state (speed, acceleration, steering angle) and higher‑level data (object tracks, projected driving path). Each data stream is serialized by the Data Translation component into ROS2-compatible messages, aligned to standardized types, and units are converted where necessary. The raw LiDAR point cloud is first passed to the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component, which processes the 2D plane representation, extracts object tracks and updates the vehicle state model. Data is transmitted over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP on the transport layer for inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity. Internally, ROS2 topics on a DDS messaging layer carry the processed data between the various SW components (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo actuation, visualization). Sensors continuously publish data, which triggers the ROS2 subscription callbacks that drive real‑time monitoring and visualization in the 3D visualization engine.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control decisions made within the Digital Twin are sent downstream to the Physical Twin using the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel. Commands comprise serialized motor actuation commands and steering servo actuation commands that specify target torque or angle values. These commands are published on ROS2 topics that map to the vehicle’s actuation interfaces. The document does not provide information on authentication, authorization, or cryptographic validation of these messages. Feedback from the vehicle’s state buses (e.g., current speed, steering angle) is fed back into the digital model to confirm command execution, but explicit mechanisms for conflict resolution or command acknowledgment are not described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin infrastructure employs a dual‑layer communication architecture. Inter‑SW component communication occurs over a ROS2 network, which aggregates messages using the DDS protocol on a local Ethernet or in‑vehicle network. For inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity, a Wi‑Fi network transmits data using UDP. The document does not specify bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols, but the choice of UDP implies a low‑latency, best‑effort transfer suitable for real‑time sensor feeds. The ROS2 layer relies on DDS Quality of Service settings (e.g., reliability, durability) to meet internal communication requirements.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system complies with ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing. It adopts the reference architecture defined in Part 2, which specifies an entity‑based hierarchy of Data Collection & Device Control, Digital Twin and User entities, each composed of functional entities. The implementation may leverage OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data collection, device control, and information exchange, thereby ensuring interoperability with standard industrial communication protocols.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
